A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like `get_keys_on_hsm()`, `get_hsms()` seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyCKS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable (optional if there are reasonable defaults for some systems)
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `"hms:?slot=c9&device=0"`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
The remaining functionality would then be `export_pubkey()`. Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could?

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

Left also some code comments -- they may be obsolete so feel free to disregard

Some general comments about the approach:

I think, we need to follow closely the current workflow approach. For example, the same test run from `tests-cpu-kernels/test_cpuawkward_IndexedArray64_validity.py` clearly shows what are the input values for each of the 25 tests it would:
```python
def test_cpuawkward_IndexedArray64_validity_2():
    index = [1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]
    index = (ctypes.c_int64*len(index))(*index)
    length = 3
    lencontent = 2
    isoption = False
    funcC = getattr(lib, 'awkward_IndexedArray64_validity')
    ret_pass = funcC(index, length, lencontent, isoption)
    assert not ret_pass.str
```
The advantage of this is that it is clear which test values cause an error. For example, if I modify it to something non-valid:
```python

    isoption = True
    funcC = getattr(lib, 'awkward_IndexedArray64_validity')
    ret_pass = funcC(index, length, lencontent, isoption)
    assert not ret_pass.str
```

**Conclusion**
Proposed approach gives us a possibility to run twice as many tests without increasing testing time. This is great! However, we should discuss how it scales with the number of roles since not all tests would have the same number of inputs. Perhaps, the json test data fragment relevant to the test should be incorporated in the test itself. The generated test could look like:
```python
import kernels

def test_awkward_IndexedArray_validity_1():
        input = [
                [0],
                10,
                1,
                False
        ]
        output = None
        result = kernels.awkward_IndexedArray_validity(*tuple(input))
        assert result == output
```

The reordering of imports is unfortunate, but this looks good overall.

Unfortunately compatibility with `typing` is a bit cursed: `from __future__ import annotations` is in [limbo](https://docs.python.org/3.11/library/__future__.html#id2) (note the lack of resolution for 3.12). If that ever gets removed, we need to make sure we use that version's types at runtime. We'll just have to take this as we can. A good next step would be adding `mypy` to `ci.sh` to run it on every platform we test (other than PyPy ones, of course).

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that `c.session.Accept()` blocks until `c.session.Close()` is called, which happens during `c.Close()`, but `c.Close()` itself also locks `c.m`, so we have to unlock `c.m` while `c.session.Accept()` is running, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, leaking deadlocked go routines, but functionality would not have been impacted, only performance.

The only other high level feedback I have is that introducing an interface type `Value` will lose some type-safety.
The way this is written, the types `BoolValue` and `StringValue` present the type of the underlying value (`bool` or `string`). Once those instances are bound to a local of type `Value` (an interface type), you'll have to ascertain the type of the underlying value using the `type()` method to error-check you've got a flag of the right type. Checking the `type()` method is going to introduce some boilerplate and requires a runtime check. If you eliminate the `Variable` interface entirely, then users are forced to work with `BoolValue` and `StringValue` directly, which requires them to know how they plan to use the flag ahead of time.

If you're using the `interface` type to reduce library boilerplate, then you might be able to use a `Variable` type with a generic argument for `Bool` and `String`.

Hey Brian,

I checked the full history of `taskcluster-proxy` in the monorepo, and `taskcluster-proxy` in its original repository, and this appears never to have been the behaviour of `taskcluster-proxy` in the past.

TLDR; I think this is the wrong place to make the fix.

I think whatever broke, broke somewhere else, and this fix may end up causing us more pain than it solves. For example, it looks like this will set the `Content-Type` header for requests that have no body, such as GET requests, which could introduce adverse affects in middleware or caching proxies etc. I think passing through only the headers that are sent, plus the Authorization header, is the correct approach, and I suspect whatever was calling taskcluster-proxy is the thing that broke, rather than taskcluster-proxy itself.

I realise this might sound like I'm splitting hairs, but it feels like this change will mask problems elsewhere and could lead to unexpected results. For example, calling code that doesn't set the header when it runs through the proxy, wouldn't work if it was pointed at the taskcluster endpoint directly. The clients should all set the `Content-Type` header when it is required, so if a client is broken and no longer setting it, we have a different problem that this change would mask, but would still exist if the same client was used calling a taskcluster service directly.

I think the next steps for us is to do a deep dive to understand what is not passing the `Content-Type` header in a request to the proxy (whether it is something in a task, something in docker-worker, or something in a taskcluster client) and hopefully we can isolate the component that introduces the issue, and find the commit that broke it, and hopefully find the smoking gun! ☁️ 🔫

I have a few comments and thoughts:
1) Maybe we should move this to dev_1.6.0 instead of dev_1.5.1, since this does break backwards compatibility with the `set_learning_phase` function. External code that currently calls it will fail. An alternative that would make things break less would be to add `set_learning_phase` as a no-op in the base class art.estimators.BaseEstimator or NeuralNetworkMixin that has a deprecation warning associated with it. (You can say that it will be removed in 1.6.0, for instance.) Something like this:
```
    @deprecated(end_version="1.6.0", replaced_by="kwarg 'training_mode' in 'class_gradient' and 'loss_gradient' methods")
    def set_learning_phase(self, train: bool) -> None:                                           
        pass
```
2) For wrapper-type classes, like detection/evasion/detector.BinaryActivationDetector and classification ensemble, internal calls to class_gradient and loss_gradient need to pass `**kwargs` through so that if the underlying classifier takes the training_mode kwarg, it gets properly passed through.
3) We may want to consider adding the "training_mode" parameter to predict, as that would be consistent with the way TF2 is now operating. Plus, you may want to be able to easily compare predictions with and without something like batchnorm or dropout. Similarly on expectation.py and potentially others.

5) Is there a rationale for placing a lot of the functionality in the classifier classes instead of the estimator base classes?

One change I would propose structurally is moving the "Choosing a Compute Engine + Which engine should I use?" sections right after "Installation" and before "Pandas API Coverage". I think this makes more logical sense since we discuss installing with different engines anyhow. 

Additionally, for the sake of simplicity for the README I propose removing the "Advanced Usage" subsection concerning initializing Ray and Dask with custom resource constraints. Resource constrained installations are covered by https://github.com/modin-project/modin/pull/3965/files as part of `using_modin_locally` in the documentation and I don't see a need for it in the README.

I also suggest retitling the "Full Documentation" section to "More about Modin". I also think we should retitle the "More information and Getting Involved" section at the end to "General Resources and Getting Involved", and adding links to tutorials, blog posts, and other interesting links that would then be easily accessible directly from the README.
I'm not happy about the built-in pitfalls this has, but it's better than what was there before, and I don't want to start on yet another API redesign.

I'm OK with the direction this PR is taking. `ModelChain.losses_model` becomes an identifier for a set of loss models. Although here only a DC loss model is added, a set can grow to include irradiance, shading, soiling, etc. The set approach seems to be a practical strategy since loss models can overlap or conflict in different implementations (e.g., shading can be modeled as a reduction in irradiance, or could be accounted for by a factor applied to DC power).

Thanks for the PR @timkpaine, this is a monstrous effort that is much appreciated.  I'm excited for the zero-config `perspective-python`/Jupyter integration future.

A couple of things stick out that I'd like you to elaborate on -

* I understand the reasoning behind renaming `@finos/perspective-jupyterlab` -> `@finos/perspective-jupyter`, but not the impact it will have on existing Jupyter users with the former already installed.  IMO this is _really important_, as the nature of this PR is to simplify installation, we don't want to co-introduce _arbitrary_ regressions.  If you think this is really important to stay, we should add correct upper bounds to the old package and publish it (rather than delete this package),  so that current users of the old package do not suddenly get deprecation errors on update, but rather a clear version-bound error saying that `@finos/perspective-jupyterlab` is incompat with the `perspective-python` bounds.  I believe (?) we can set the NPM deprecation message on that version to point to the new package.
* Please, please, for my own sanity - do not introduce `webpack` back into our build process, especially not to generate a full inline build _twice_.  I _finally_ managed to remove `webpack` entirely in favor of `esbuild`.  Even for a less-frequently-updated package like this, it is disruptive in the extreme, and Jupyter itself already imposes its own build tax on our precious development time.  I need to dig in a bit more (if you want me to), but I see no reason this can't be built in `esbuild` with the existing plugins in worst case;  but what would likely be even easier is to just import the pre-built inline builds of `@finos/perspective` and `@finos/perspective-viewer` directly, then `esbuild` these.  As they are pure JavaScript already, you'll need no special plugins, get an almost identical bundle, at 1000x the build performance.  The only quirk I see is specifying `externals` correctly?

smoothing difference between `0.99` and `0.999`, and I often like to set

value logarithmically: say, apply `log_2(1 - x)` to the current value.

**A Theory**

I have a theory about what's going on. I think that it might be the case that once early stopping has been detected, workers are shutting down and not carefully coordinating the shutdown. That could create a race condition where the order that they shut down matters. I suspect this because you were getting `socket recv error: 104`.

That comes from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/e5c3f7e755659c120b94c109b515569fa6765b9c/src/network/socket_wrapper.hpp#L286, and the specific integer value comes from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/e5c3f7e755659c120b94c109b515569fa6765b9c/src/network/socket_wrapper.hpp#L168. I saw answers on Stack Overflow saying that 104 means connection reset by peer, and based on https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/errno.h.html I can confirm that's true. You could run the code below on http://cpp.sh/ (or locally with the C++ compiler of your choice) to confirm.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cmath>
#include <cerrno>
#include <cstring>
#include <clocale>
#include <cstdio>
 
int main()
{
    std::cout << "message: " << std::strerror(ECONNRESET) << '\n';
    printf("error code: %d\n", ECONNRESET);
}
```

> message: Connection reset by peer
error code: 104

I think this type of problem could explain all three behaviors you're seeing:

* sometimes it works
* sometimes training fails with this "connection reset by peer"
* sometimes it hangs forever

We could try to figure this out by looking at how [mmlspark](https://github.com/Azure/mmlspark) handles early stopping, and by `@`-ing in other maintainers here. We could also try to see if the problem shows up in non-Dask distributed training, like with the LightGBM CLI, by picking up #3841.

HOWEVER, before we do that, I have a request.

**A Request**

Can you open a separate PR with only the non-early-stopping eval changes? Being able to use evaluation sets is valuable even without early stopping, because that allows you to get a deeper understanding of how the training process is progressing. The `test_eval_set_without_early_stopping` tests passed every time I ran them, so I'm fairly confident that they could be separated out.

```shell
pytest test_dask.py::test_eval_set_without_early_stopping
```

Doing the evaluation sets separately would also make this PR smaller and make it a bit easier for us to focus in on the source of instability uncovered by your tests.

Thank you SO SO SO much for all of your hard work on this. Sorry the PR has dragged on for a while. Now that the next release is almost ready, I can be a lot more responsive and help move this forward.

I don't think local template manager should be made aware of tkg plus flag.

Infact this current change will revert your recent fix, for the tkg templates. i.e. templates will be left without being tagged with proper compute profile, neither any error would be raised.

I understand that by moving the filtering logic out of ltm, we would be duplicating the filtering logic in few places. But, we don't want to miss processing templates because the flag was turned off during upgrade.

Example scenario.
CSE server was upgrade to v3.0 at api v33.0, with tkg+ enabled. TKG+ template was created too.

Thanks for the PR @oke-aditya! About your questions:

1. IMO this basically reduces to: do we allow batch processing or not. Given that we cant parallelize this due to our `PIL` usage I'd say we don't allow it. Passing batches would internally mean we use a for loop anyway and thus the user has no real advantage. I think we can simply `try` to `squeeze(0)` if we encounter an image with 4 dimensions and handle the error if this fails.
2. Yes, we should return the tensor. Why wouldn't we or more importantly: why would a user use this function if he gets no results back?
3. It can only be transparent if we use a forth channel for the alpha, i.e. change the image from `RGB` to `RGBA`. I'm not sure if this is a good idea. Is it common to fill the bounding boxes? I've never seen it before, which does not mean it does not exist. Otherwise I see no point in adding a `fill` option.
4. I agree testing this will not be easy, but I think we can cover some basic stuff. For example you can start with an all white image, draw a bounding box and check if only the pixels you would expect are changed. You can also test the colors with this. 

In addition to your questions, I have some other remarks below. The linter is also failing, but lets postpone this until we have the functionality right.

import warnings

Thanks for the PR @brianblakely!  It's a much-needed addition and much-appreciated contribution!

The content here is good!   It covers the important points and there are some really nice example formulas especially.   I'd like to see the structure updated a bit to make it clearer to read, and easier for us to maintain, namely:

1) Let's rely-on/defer-to the [ExprTK docs](https://github.com/ArashPartow/exprtk/blob/master/readme.txt) as much as possible for core functionality coverage, as it does a more thorough job at no cost to us.  Where we're re-iterating information contained there, I'd prefer these explanations be grouped in a sections entitled something like "ExprTK Core Quickstart" or similar, so there is a clear distinction between this and ...
2) ... A dedicated section for _just_ the non-native extensions we've made to ExprTK, namely the extended scalar types (dates/strings/bools/etc), type inference system, column reference syntax (double-quotes), column alias via comments, and single quote string literals.  Not everything has to be covered right away in this PR, the key is to organize what's already written re: these extensions, separately from the core language quickstart.
3) Please move any additions you want to make to the our added core-library functions themselves to to Rust metadata statics (as per the inline comment re: autogeneration+intellisense). 
4) Try to remove the image assets (that are missing from this PR anyway) - you can replace them with CSS highlights of an embedded `<perspective-viewer>` if you're feeling adventurous, or just remove them.  We are trying to put the project on a diet of debt like this.

Side note - the example expressions are good, I'd love to see a `/examples/blocks` demo of a number of these expressions as applied to `superstore.arrow` so we can make it a `README.md` gallery example highlighting different expressions ... maybe a topic for a future PR?

```
tests/test_query.py::test_should_reduce_number_of_queries_by_using_select_related FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_optimize_when_using_fragments FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_select_nested_related_fields FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_prefetch_nested_select_related_field FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_select_nested_prefetch_related_field FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_select_nested_prefetch_and_select_related_fields FAILED
tests/test_query.py::test_should_fetch_fields_of_related_field FAILED
tests/test_relay.py::test_should_reduce_number_of_queries_in_relay_schema_by_using_select_related FAILED
```

For instance, for `test_should_reduce_number_of_queries_by_using_select_related`:

```python
>       assert_query_equality(items, optimized_items)

tests/test_query.py:39:
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _

left_query = <QuerySet []>, right_query = <QuerySet []>

    def assert_query_equality(left_query, right_query):
>       assert str(left_query.query) == str(right_query.query)
E       assert 'SELECT "test...."name" = bar' == 'SELECT "tests...."name" = bar'
E         - SELECT "tests_item"."id", "tests_item"."name", "tests_item"."parent_id", "tests_item"."item_id", "tests_item"."value" FROM "tests_item" WHERE "tests_item"."name" = bar
E         + SELECT "tests_item"."id", "tests_item"."name", "tests_item"."parent_id", "tests_item"."item_id", "tests_item"."value", T2."id", T2."name", T2."parent_id", T2."item_id", T2."value" FROM "tests_item" LEFT OUTER JOIN "tests_item" T2 ON ("tests_item"."parent_id" = T2."id") WHERE "tests_item"."name" = bar
```

It looks to me like the `select_related` optimization is getting removed. Maybe a quirk of how those tests are written and how this `CustomField` override is happening? Any ideas how to fix that?

First of all, yay! Thanks for making our Lambda support miles better. 

The implementation looks great to me. In response to your question:

It does seem weird for a layer to have an entry point, and I didn't quite get your point about macros, or why in your example the layer would care about foo.py at all (assuming that is the entry point). Surely the layer doesn't know about entry points that use it?

It seems like "a lambda uses a layer" is modeled by a dep, not an entry point. And it would be great for Pants to provide lambda publishing that prevents the user from having to generate metadata that just recapitulates these dependencies.

But there are a few caveats:

1) Order does matter for layers - they are merged in the order in which they are specified in the function's metadata, and so to create that metadata we would have to preserve the order. Does the dep field preserve order? 

2) A function can only have 5 layers (not sure if that includes the function itself).

3) There's no transitivity - a layer can't depend on other layers. 

So we'd have to do some validation if we model these as deps. The alternative is to give the lambda target a field that is a flat list of layers. 

And in the mix is dep inference - is that likely to be possible or useful here?

```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";

Then run `pulumi preview --save-plan plan.json` then immediately `pulumi up --plan plan.json`, I get:

```

Previewing update (dev)

View Live: https://app.pulumi.com/lukehoban/simple-foo/dev/previews/66981c6d-e229-45ae-a086-a010d3e0c0b5

     Type                 Name            Plan
     pulumi:pulumi:Stack  simple-foo-dev
     └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket

Resources:
    2 unchanged

➜  simple PULUMI_EXPERIMENTAL=true /opt/pulumi/bin/pulumi up --plan plan.json
Previewing update (dev)

 ~   └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket       refresh

Diagnostics:
  pulumi:pulumi:Stack (simple-foo-dev):
    error: resource violates plan: properties changed: -bucketName


error: Expected resource operations for urn:pulumi:dev::simple-foo::pulumi:pulumi:Stack::simple-foo-dev but none were seen.
```

Second - an issue that I suspect is due to the refresh piece (I have notes in the review feedback that I think we need to decouple this - but if we are considering keeping this, we'll need to ensure it doesn't trigger the issue here):

If I deploy this:

```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";
const bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("my-bucket", {});
```

And then change it to:

```ts
```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";
const bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("my-bucket", {
    website: {
        indexDocument: "index.html",
    },
});
```

Then run `pulumi preview --save-plan plan.json` then immediately `pulumi up --plan plan.json`, I get:

```
➜  simple PULUMI_EXPERIMENTAL=true /opt/pulumi/bin/pulumi up --plan plan.json
Previewing update (dev)

View Live: https://app.pulumi.com/lukehoban/simple-foo/dev/previews/cf933799-a8ec-4d61-9fe6-a73232576220

     Type                 Name            Plan        Info
     pulumi:pulumi:Stack  simple-foo-dev
 ~   └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket       refresh     1 error

Diagnostics:
  aws:s3:Bucket (my-bucket):
    error: resource violates plan: properties changed: -acl, -websiteEndpoint, -bucketDomainName, -requestPayer, -bucketRegionalDomainName, -corsRules, -tags, -region, -lifecycleRules, -versioning, -tagsAll, -bucket, -loggings, -websiteDomain, -id, -forceDestroy, -accelerationStatus, -grants, -hostedZoneId, -arn, +website


error: Expected resource operations for urn:pulumi:dev::simple-foo::aws:s3/bucket:Bucket::my-bucket but none were seen.
```

All of these changed properties are results of the refresh modifying the state, but don't seem to be captured into the plan, so that the plan doesn't actually capture what the combined operation is going to do, so the corresponding update can't work.

The main question I have is why we need a new `Restorable` interface, when most of the functionality is provided by python already.

I.e. there is `obj.__getstate__()` and `obj.__setstate__()`, and it's automatically used for pickling and unpickling. Shouldn't users just implement those?

For the callback list or other locations where we pickle callbacks, we can catch serialization errors and provide an actionable error message.

It feels like we're trying to solve a problem that's already solved. If we went with `Restorable`, would the trial runner, searcher, etc also eventually inherit this interface?

https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/CumulusCI/blob/46ae50dcbe404f443fe9da7d70074cb398ab47a1/cumulusci/core/config/sfdx_org_config.py#L169

Without using `shlex.split` or its equivalent we won't have any options (args) to print. The other tricky bit is that the most common way to invoke SFDX, `SFDXOrgTask`, doesn't use `sfdx` at all, it inherits from `SFDXBaseTask`:

(🐢³)

I attempted implementing this in the past, back when Travis CI was a thing. And there were flaky problems with certain envs so we reverted this in the end. Earlier this year, I tried again. I managed to address many problems but not all. See https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/5431 for that last try.

TL;DR The I/O loop must **always** run in the main thread in order for the signal handling to work. With pytest-xdist it's not always the case because sometimes the underlying lib `execnet` occupies that main thread with something else in an inconsistent manner. It now happens very rarely and is hard to reproduce — on my laptop, I had to make like 12-15 terminal tabs and invoke parallelized pytest in all of them (almost) simultaneously. Please follow the pointers in https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/5431 for more details.

In my last draft (https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/5431), I've tried auto-enabling pytest-xdist automatically under Python3.8+ with a new watcher that is capable of handling signals in threads. It is now on hold as I don't have time to continue exploring the problem at the moment but feel free to cherry-pick my commits into your branch (and maybe rebase on top) and try to check how well that'd work by overloading your system with many parallel processes and threads (that underlying race condition is most likely happen in such an env). Also, make sure to transfer any explanations/pointers from my PR so that all the info is consolidated here.

Lastly, I wanted to mention that I like the approach of having a separate make target. Ideally, though, the parallelized test run should be made default at some point and used in CI where any speed-ups are very welcome as well.

> @majorgreys so I argue that we don't need to go the feature flag route just because the fallback logic here is equivalent to what we had before so there would be no need to downgrade.

But the fallback flow is:

1. -- First request --
   1. Create a connection
   2. Send a request
   3. Try to keep connection alive
4. -- Second request --
  1. Try to reuse previous connection
  2. Connection fails, clear connection, raise an exception
  3. Tenacity retries
  4. Create a new connection
  5. Make request
  6. Try to keep connection alive
3. -- each further request --
  1. Same as for the second request


Is this correct? Is so, then the fallback logic is to raise an exception, then have Tenacity do a back-off retry, and then a successful request? Which is fine for a fail-safe, but a feature flag we can short circuit that exception -> retry logic for every single request.
